feedback.text: I would like to respectfully urge you to consider adding a sixth unfair practice under the Digital Fairness Act: the permanent disabling or removal of access to video games that consumers have legally purchased. In recent years, we have seen a disturbing trend in the digital entertainment industry. Publishers and platform holders have shut down servers, delisted digital-only games, or discontinued access to games entirelyeven for users who already paid for them. This practice is becoming increasingly common as more games require online activation, server-side components, or are sold exclusively through digital storefronts. A few well-known examples come to mind: The closure of the PlayStation Store for older systems (such as PS3, PSP, and PS Vita) has made dozensif not hundredsof titles inaccessible. Nintendo shutting down its eShop for the 3DS and Wii U, leaving many digital-only games impossible to obtain or re-download. Online-only games being removed from storefronts and rendered completely unplayable after server shutdowns. In each of these cases, consumers who paid for a product were left with nothing to show for it. The economic harm is clear: players lose access to content they purchased, and the second-hand market or re-purchasing options are often nonexistent. But there is also a cultural harm here that I believe should not be ignored. When publishers delist or disable video games permanently, we are not just losing consumer productswe are losing pieces of digital heritage. Destroying a video game in this manner is akin to burning a book, erasing a painting, or destroying sheet music. Games are cultural artifacts, and many represent unique creative visions, technological innovations, and artistic achievements. Imagine if a publishing house had the legal power to break into your home and confiscate the books you've already boughtbecause the company no longer wanted to support them. That is effectively what is happening when digital rights management and publisher policies allow games to be erased retroactively, with no recourse for those who already purchased them. Personally, I have experienced the frustration of returning to a game I loved in my youth, only to discover it is no longer availablenot digitally, not physically, not anywhere. In many cases, the only option left is piracy, which leaves law-abiding customers punished while those willing to break the rules retain access. That cannot be considered fair or sustainable. Therefore, I respectfully suggest the Digital Fairness Act include language to prohibit publishers from permanently disabling access to video games that have already been sold to consumers, or at the very least to mandate the preservation of functionality through offline modes, patches, or the release of server software to the community. I believe this step is not only necessary to protect consumer rights but also to preserve our digital cultural heritage. Thank you for your time and consideration.
